questions.statement: Directive Principles of State Policy are stated in Part IV of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Directive Principles are stated in Articles 36 to 51, in Part IV of the Constitution.

questions.statement: Directive Principles of State Policy are justiciable and can be enforced by courts.
questions.explanation: Directive Principles are non-justiciable, meaning they are NOT enforceable by any court of law.

questions.statement: The Directive Principles were inspired by the Constitution of Ireland.
questions.explanation: The Directive Principles were inspired by the Directive Principles stated in the Constitution of Ireland and principles of Gandhian philosophy.

questions.statement: Primary education has been made compulsory for children up to the age of 14 years.
questions.explanation: This is one of the Directive Principles promoting social and economic equality.

questions.statement: The state shall not take steps to separate the Judiciary from the Executive.
questions.explanation: The state shall take steps to separate the Judiciary from the Executive, not avoid doing so.

questions.statement: The state shall promote international peace and security.
questions.explanation: Promoting international peace and security is a General Directive Principle.

questions.statement: Fundamental Rights guarantee political democracy.
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights guarantee political democracy, while Directive Principles establish social and economic democracy.

questions.statement: The state shall not take steps to promote agriculture.
questions.explanation: Under Gandhian Directive Principles, the state shall take steps to improve the quality of livestock and promote agriculture.

questions.statement: Fundamental Rights are protected by the Supreme Court and High Courts.
questions.explanation: Fundamental Rights are justiciable and enforceable, protected by the Supreme Court and High Courts.

questions.statement: The state shall promote the educational and economic interests of the weaker sections of the society.
questions.explanation: Under Gandhian Principles, the state shall promote the educational and economic interests of weaker sections, especially Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes.

questions.statement: Under British rule, India was a welfare state.
questions.explanation: Under British rule, India was NOT a welfare state. The British were not interested in the welfare of the Indian people.

questions.statement: A welfare state is based on the principles of equal distribution of wealth.
questions.explanation: A welfare state is based on principles of equality of opportunity and equal distribution of wealth.

questions.statement: Directive Principles and Fundamental Rights are contradictory to each other.
questions.explanation: They are NOT contradictory but complementary. Both aim at the same thing - the welfare of citizens.

questions.statement: The Zamindari system was abolished as an impact of the Directive Principles.
questions.explanation: The Zamindari system was abolished and surplus land of landlords was distributed among the landless poor.

questions.statement: Untouchability has been made a punishable offence.
questions.explanation: Untouchability was made a punishable offence as one of the positive impacts of Directive Principles.

questions.statement: The state shall try to promote large-scale industries in rural areas.
questions.explanation: The state shall try to promote cottage industries (not large-scale industries) in rural areas, as per Gandhian Directive Principles.

questions.statement: Swaraj means welfare for all.
questions.explanation: Swaraj means self-rule, not welfare for all. Sarvodaya means welfare for all.

questions.statement: The 42nd Amendment added the duty to protect the environment as a Directive Principle.
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment added a new directive - the duty of the state to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country.

questions.statement: Directive Principles help in protecting human rights.
questions.explanation: According to the textbook, Directive Principles help in protecting human rights, while Fundamental Rights are natural rights.

questions.statement: The state should distribute wealth so it is concentrated in a few hands.
questions.explanation: The state should distribute wealth so it is NOT concentrated in a few hands. This is a Directive Principle promoting social and economic equality.
